articles.title: Federal Reserve of Waterdeep Announces Interest Rate Hike Amidst Goblin Inflation
articles.author: Gimble Inkquill
articles.excerpt: In a move that has sent shockwaves through the merchant districts, the High Lords of Finance have raised the cost of borrowing gold pieces by 2.5%.
articles.content:
In a move that has sent shockwaves through the merchant districts, the High Lords of Finance have raised the cost of borrowing gold pieces by 2.5%. The decision comes as the price of basic healing potions has skyrocketed due to a shortage of glass vials and a goblin cartel controlling the silica trade.


"It's a necessary correction," stated Lord Candlekeep, Chief Economist. "We cannot allow the value of the Platinum Piece to be diluted by reckless spending on royal banquets."


Critics, however, argue that the real culprit is the recent dragon migration, which has disrupted supply lines and incinerated several key grain silos. Commoners are reportedly hoarding copper pieces and trading in barter systems involving livestock and +1 Swords.

articles.title: Kingdom Passes 'Stand Your Ground' Shield Law
articles.author: Elara Swiftquill
articles.excerpt: Citizens now legally allowed to bash first, ask questions later. Opposition claims this violates the Treaty of Non-Aggression.
articles.content: The Royal Council has passed controversial legislation allowing citizens to use defensive force without retreating first. Critics argue this will lead to more tavern brawls.

articles.title: Unicorn Start-Up Valuation Plummets
articles.author: Marcus Goldhand
articles.excerpt: Investors realize unicorns don't actually produce gold, just sparkles. Stock drops 40% in morning trading.
articles.content: The mythical creature market took a hit today as investors realized the fundamental flaw in unicorn-based economies.

articles.title: Dungeon Delvers Unionize
articles.author: Thorin Ironforge
articles.excerpt: "We demand hazard pay for traps," says union rep. "And dental for when we bite into cursed coins."
articles.content: The Adventurers Guild Local 421 has voted to strike until better working conditions are met.

articles.title: New Teleportation Circle App Glitches
articles.author: Zara Spellweaver
articles.excerpt: Users report arriving 5 feet to the left of their destination, frequently inside walls. Developers call it a 'feature.'
articles.content: TeleportCorp's new app has users stuck in walls. The company says it's working on a patch.

articles.title: High Wizard Fined for Unauthorized Tower Expansion
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: Planning committee claims the extra dimension violates spatial zoning laws. Wizard argues 'it's bigger on the inside' is a loophole.
articles.content: The Arcane Zoning Board has issued a cease and desist order after Bigby's tower exceeded its dimensional footprint. Neighbors complain of gravity leaks and occasional time dilation in the parking lot.

articles.title: Archmage Joebid and Warlord Donwald Clash in Debate Arena
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: The magical microphones amplified every stumble. Voters worry about the vitality of the elders versus the chaos of the golden tower.
articles.content:
The Great Debate Arena of Waterdeep was shaken to its foundational stones last night as the two leading contenders for the High Seat of the Republic exchanged verbal fireballs in a spectacle that left many bystanders questioning the sanity of the entire electoral process. Archmage Joebid the Elder, representing the Party of the Blue Donkey, took the stage leaning heavily on his staff of office, occasionally confusing the name of the current kingdom with one that fell centuries ago. His opponents claim he is under the influence of a slow-acting confusion spell, though his allies insist he is merely conserving mana for the final campaign push.

Opposing him stood Warlord Donwald the Golden, a figure clad in robes of excessive length and a mane of hair that defies gravity magic. Donwald spent much of the evening shouting over the magical mute buttons, claiming the moderators were goblins in disguise sent to sabotage his rally. He promised to build a wall of force around the southern borders of the realm to keep out the barbarian hordes, though critics note the wall already exists and is merely made of parchment regulations. The audience, composed of undecided voters from the Swing States of the Midlands, appeared visibly exhausted by the third hour, many resorting to casting minor illusion spells to make themselves appear elsewhere.

Polls conducted by the Beholder's Gazette immediately following the event show a split populace. Half believe the realm needs the steady, if sleepy, hand of Joebid, while the other half crave the chaotic energy of Donwald's golden towers. Meanwhile, the third-party candidates, including a Druid who promises to turn the capital into a forest and a Libertarian who wants to abolish the concept of money, were barred from the stage for failing to meet the polling thresholds. The next debate is scheduled to take place in a volcano, because apparently, the political climate was not hot enough already.

Security wizards reported confiscating several cursed amulets from the audience that were designed to make the wearer laugh uncontrollably at serious policy statements. The Election Commission has assured the public that the voting golems will be calibrated correctly, despite rumors that some were found counting ballots in reverse order. As the campaign season drags on like a never-ending questline, the common folk of Waterdeep are left wondering if either candidate actually possesses the spell required to fix the potholes on the King's Road.

articles.title: Czar Vlad the Bear-Handed Freezes Trade Routes
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: The Order of the Western Shield imposes new sanctions. Czar Vlad claims the cold weather is a natural phenomenon, not a weaponized spell.
articles.content:
Tensions in the frozen wastes of Damara have reached a boiling point, or rather, a freezing point, as Czar Vlad the Bear-Handed continues his expansionist campaign into the neighboring sovereign duchies. The Czar, who rules from a fortress made entirely of obsidian and intimidation, claims that the neighboring lands are historically part of his ancestral lair, despite maps drawn by ancient cartographers suggesting otherwise. He has deployed legions of ice golems and winter wizards to secure the region, causing trade routes through the mountain passes to become impassable for merchant caravans. The merchants' guild of Waterdeep is screaming bloody murder as the price of imported furs and iron ore skyrockets beyond the reach of the average adventurer.

In response, the Order of the Western Shield, a coalition of kingdoms led by the High Lords of America and Cormyr, has enacted a series of crippling sanctions. These magical embargoes prevent the Czar's allies from accessing global banking networks and purchasing high-level enchantments. However, spies report that Vlad has established secret trade deals with the Dragon Empires of the East, exchanging stolen grain for advanced weaponry and protection charms. The common soldiers on the front lines are said to be fighting with equipment from the last age, while the generals feast on delicacies in bunkers deep underground.

Diplomatic envoys attempting to negotiate a peace treaty have been turned into newts, a classic diplomatic maneuver attributed to Vlad's court wizards. The High Council of Nations is debating whether to send direct military aid in the form of mercenary companies or to rely on economic pressure. Meanwhile, the peasants of Damara suffer under the weight of conscription, forced to leave their farms to fight in a war many do not understand. Propaganda scrolls distributed in the capital claim victory is imminent, though refugees fleeing the border tell tales of villages erased by fireballs.

Environmental druids have noted that the unnatural cold spreading from the Czar's territory is affecting the migration patterns of the local griffins. They warn that if the magical balance of the region is not restored, the spring thaw may never come, leading to a permanent winter that could threaten the food supply of the entire continent. For now, the world watches and waits, hoping the Czar's ambition does not outpace his supply of mana crystals.

articles.title: Federal Reserve Prints Too Much Gold Leaf
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: The price of healing potions doubles. Merchant Princes blame supply chain goblins; Wizards blame greed.
articles.content:
The economy of the realms is in a state of chaotic flux as the Federal Reserve of Waterdeep continues to print gold leaf at an alarming rate, causing the value of the standard gold piece to plummet faster than a rogue with a failed levitate spell. Commoners visiting the marketplaces report that the price of basic necessities, such as bread, milk, and healing potions, has doubled in the last quarter alone. The Merchant Princes of Amn, who control the majority of the trade networks, claim that the inflation is due to supply chain goblins sabotaging the caravans, though many economists suspect it is simply a case of too much magic chasing too few goods.

Wizard of Social Gold Bernie Sanders has taken to the town squares, preaching that the hoard of wealth held by the top one percent of dragon-kings should be redistributed to the struggling peasantry. He argues that no single individual should possess enough gold to buy a small kingdom while children go hungry in the streets. The Merchant Princes counter that such redistribution would violate the sacred laws of capitalism and cause the economy to collapse into a heap of rusted copper. They threaten to move their operations to the untaxed zones of the Outer Planes if taxes are raised any higher.

Meanwhile, the housing market has become completely inaccessible to the average adventurer. The cost of renting a small room in a safe neighborhood now requires a monthly income equivalent to slaying a mature dragon. Young mages are forced to live in their parents' basements well into their forties, delaying their quests for glory and marriage. The High Lord of the Realm has promised to address the issue, but his proposed solutions involve complex financial instruments that sound suspiciously like illusion spells designed to make the numbers look better without actually changing anything.

Local alchemists are struggling to source ingredients for their potions as the cost of herbs and minerals rises daily. Some have resorted to using substitute ingredients that have unpleasant side effects, such as turning the drinker's skin blue for a week. The unrest is growing, with protests forming outside the banks where the gold is minted. The city guard has been placed on high alert, fearing that the frustration of the populace might boil over into riots. Until the flow of gold is stabilized, the common folk remain trapped in a dungeon of their own economic making.

articles.title: Lady Hillara Summoned by Inquisitors Over Scrolls
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: Boxes of ancient secrets found in a dungeon basement. She claims they were souvenirs; the Inquisitor claims it's treason.
articles.content:
The legal dungeons of Waterdeep are echoing with the sounds of a high-profile inquisition as Lady Hillara of the Deleted Scrolls faces questioning regarding the classified grimoires found in her private tower. The Special Inquisitor Smith has been appointed to determine whether the Lady intentionally hoarded state secrets for personal gain or if she was merely disorganized, a common affliction among high-level casters. Boxes containing spells of national security were discovered in her basement storage room, mixed in with old Christmas decorations and broken wands, raising questions about the security protocols of the former Archmage.

Lady Hillara maintains that she had the authority to keep the scrolls as souvenirs of her time in office, arguing that she declassified them with a mere thought, though no written record of this mental decree exists. Her legal team, composed of the most expensive barristers in the realm, argues that the investigation is a politically motivated hex designed to prevent her from seeking higher office in the future. They claim that similar actions by Warlord Donwald are being ignored, pointing to a double standard in the enforcement of the kingdom's secrecy laws. The public remains divided, with some viewing her as a victim of a witch hunt and others as a noble who believes herself above the law.

The Inquisitor has called upon several witnesses, including former aides who claim they warned the Lady about the improper storage of the magical texts. Some testimony suggests that a private server was used to transmit sensitive information, bypassing the secure crystal ball network established for official business. The potential penalties range from a heavy fine to permanent exile from the casting of arcane magic. The trial has become a spectacle, with vendors selling popcorn and commemorative robes outside the courthouse.

Security experts warn that the presence of these scrolls in an unsecured location poses a risk to the realm, as enemy spies could have accessed them during the time they were missing. The Intelligence Guild is currently auditing all former officials to ensure no other magical artifacts have wandered off into private hands. Meanwhile, Lady Hillara continues to campaign for support, framing the legal battle as a fight for the soul of the bureaucracy. The verdict is expected before the next harvest, though delays are likely as both sides file motions to summon additional demons as witnesses.

articles.title: Grand Vizier Mitch Casts Time Stop Spell Again
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: The Senate chamber is frozen in time. The Party of the Blue Donkey demands a vote; the Party of the Red Elephant demands silence.
articles.content:
The Senate Chamber of Waterdeep has once again fallen victim to the dreaded Time Stop spell, cast by Grand Vizier Mitch the Turtle to prevent a vote on the new infrastructure bill. The Vizier, known for his slow speech patterns and strategic patience, argued that the bill contained too many cursed provisions that would burden the taxpayers for generations. By invoking the ancient right of the filibuster, he successfully halted the progression of time within the chamber, leaving the opposing party screaming into a void where no votes could be cast. The spectacle lasted for three days, during which the Vizier read from a book of phone numbers to fill the silence.

Speaker Nancy Pelosis of the Blue Donkey Party expressed her frustration, stating that the obstructionism was preventing much-needed repairs to the kingdom's bridges and roads. She threatened to invoke the Nuclear Option, a ritual that would change the rules of the chamber and limit the ability of the minority to cast time spells. However, members of her own party are hesitant, fearing that such a move could backfire when they inevitably become the minority in the next election cycle. The political maneuvering has left the common legislation in limbo, with bills piling up on the clerk's desk like unpaid quests.

The Red Elephant Party, led by the Vizier, claims they are protecting the realm from reckless spending and magical overreach. They argue that the infrastructure bill is a trojan horse designed to insert social charms into the construction contracts. Their base supports the stall tactics, viewing them as a necessary defense against the expansion of the bureaucratic state. Meanwhile, the independent voters watch in bewilderment as nothing gets done while the leaders argue about the rules of the argument itself.

Outside the chamber, protesters have gathered to demand action on various issues, from climate change to student loan forgiveness. They are met by guards who inform them that the Senate is currently in a magical stasis and cannot hear their pleas. The King has expressed concern that the gridlock is damaging the reputation of the monarchy, but his powers to intervene are limited by the constitution. For now, the chamber remains frozen, a testament to the power of a single wizard to stop the progress of an entire nation with nothing but words and procedural magic.

articles.title: Candlekeep Librarians Battle Ink Imp Rebellion
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: Mischievous imps have stolen all the blue ink. Scribes are forced to write in beet juice. The Head Librarian is not amused.
articles.content:
The hallowed halls of Candlekeep, the greatest repository of knowledge in the Forgotten Realms, were thrown into disarray yesterday morning when a coordinated rebellion of ink imps swept through the scriptorium. These tiny, mischievous fiends, typically confined to the margins of cursed spellbooks, managed to escape their bindings during a routine cleansing ritual. Their target was specific and devastating: every single vial of blue ink within the fortress monastery was drained, spilled, or magically evaporated. The Head Librarian, a stern wizard known for his patience and thick spectacles, was reportedly seen turning shades of purple reminiscent of the very ink that was stolen.

Scribes across the facility were left scrambling for alternatives, resulting in a day's worth of historical records written in beet juice, crushed berry pulp, and occasionally dragon blood. While the latter provides excellent permanence, it tends to attract predatory beasts who can smell the arcane residue from miles away. Several junior acolytes reported seeing their manuscripts suddenly grow teeth and attempt to bite their quills. The magical community is baffled by the organization displayed by the imps, suggesting that there may be a ringleader, perhaps a rogue illusionist using the creatures to protest the new digitization of scrolls into crystal balls.

Security golems have been deployed to patrol the stacks, but they are having difficulty distinguishing between legitimate scholars and imps disguised as quills. There have been several incidents where a librarian attempted to dip a creature into an inkwell, only for it to fly away laughing maniacally. The maintenance staff is overwhelmed by the sheer volume of sticky blue footprints leading to the ventilation shafts. Some theorists believe this is a sign of the impending Age of Chaos, while others suggest the imps were just bored. The Head Librarian has issued a decree promising a reward of five hundred gold pieces for the capture of the imp king, dead or alive, though preferably alive so he can be forced to lick the floors clean.

In the meantime, classes on historical transcription have been cancelled. Students are instead being taught the art of charcoal rubbing, which is messier but significantly harder for imps to steal. Local merchants are reporting a surge in demand for red and green ink, causing a temporary shortage in those colors as well. The situation has become so dire that some wizards are resorting to using magic to write directly into the air, though this method is fleeting and often blown away by drafts. The Gazette recommends all visitors to Candlekeep bring their own writing supplies, preferably in sealed containers warded against tiny claws. Until the rebellion is quelled, the knowledge of the ages remains temporarily illegible.

articles.title: Ancient Red Dragon Announces Vegan Lifestyle
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: Ignis the Incinerator says meat is 'out of style'. Local farmers confused by demand for giant cabbages instead of cattle.
articles.content:
In a shocking turn of events that has baffled druids and rangers alike, Ignis the Incinerator, an ancient red dragon notorious for burning down three villages last decade, has announced he is adopting a strictly vegan lifestyle. The dragon made the announcement via a cloud of smoke shaped like a carrot over the capital city. According to his herald, a nervous kobold who speaks on his behalf, Ignis has decided that consuming sentient beings is 'bad for the complexion' and that the ethical implications of eating sheep weigh heavily on his conscience. This has left the surrounding kingdoms in a state of confused relief, as the annual tribute of cattle has been cancelled.

Local farmers are struggling to adapt to the new demand. Instead of herding cows to the dragon's lair, they are now tasked with growing colossal vegetables capable of sating a beast the size of a castle. The Guild of Agriculturists is reporting a shortage of giant cabbages and pumpkins, causing prices to skyrocket. Some farmers are attempting to enchant their crops to grow larger overnight, but this has led to incidents where the vegetables become animate and chase the harvesters. Ignis has reportedly been seen sunbathing on his hoard, which is now partially composed of rare seeds and compost rather than just gold coins and gems. He claims the fiber intake has improved his fire breath consistency.

Adventuring parties who were previously hired to slay the dragon are now being hired to protect his garden from pests. A group of level ten paladins was recently seen fighting off a horde of giant locusts threatening Ignis's kale supply. The dragon has expressed gratitude by breathing fire on the locusts, turning them into crispy snacks which he then refused to eat, giving them to the kobolds instead. Critics argue this is merely a phase or a trick to lower the guard of his enemies, but veterinary mages who have examined him confirm his digestive system has indeed changed. He is now immune to poison damage from spoiled lettuce.

The economic impact is rippling through the region. Blacksmiths who specialized in making cages for livestock are pivoting to make gardening tools. The local taverns have renamed their signature stew to 'Dragon's Garden Delight', though patrons complain it lacks protein. Ignis has started a scroll blog detailing his recipes, such as 'Flame-Grilled Asparagus' and 'Molten Lava Roasted Potatoes'. Whether this lifestyle change will last remains to be seen, but for now, the skies are safer, and the air smells slightly more like a salad bar than a barbecue. The Kingdom has officially declared him a Protected Herbivore, though they keep a ballista loaded just in case he gets a craving for steak.

articles.title: Patrons Float to Ceiling After Ale Mishap
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: The Yawning Portal's new brew causes zero-gravity effects. Bartender claims it's a feature, not a bug. Cleanup is difficult.
articles.content:
The famous Yawning Portal tavern in Waterdeep experienced a gravitational anomaly last night that left dozens of patrons stuck on the ceiling for over three hours. The incident occurred shortly after the taproom introduced a new seasonal brew called 'Cloudwalker Stout', imported from a brewery in the Elemental Plane of Air. The first few drinkers reported feeling light-headed, which they attributed to the alcohol content. However, when a burly barbarian began drifting upward like a helium balloon, panic ensued. Within minutes, the entire taproom was inverted, with customers clinging to the rafters while their chairs and tables floated gently below them.

The bartender, a seasoned dwarf named Durnan, claimed that the effect was intentional, describing it as an 'immersive aerial dining experience'. He refused to serve the antidote potion, which was kept behind the bar, until everyone ordered another round. This decision was met with mixed reviews; some patrons enjoyed the view of the dungeon entrance from above, while others were terrified of falling should the spell wear off abruptly. Several bards took advantage of the situation to perform gravity-defying acrobatics, charging extra gold for songs sung while upside down. The cleaning staff, however, is furious, as sweeping the floor now requires climbing ladders and using nets to catch falling dust bunnies.

City guards arrived to investigate but found themselves unable to enter the establishment without also floating upward. They established a perimeter below the tavern, catching anyone who slipped out the door and fell to the street. Fortunately, no injuries were reported, though several egos were bruised when knights in full plate armor realized they could not climb down without removing their gear. A team of wizards was eventually summoned to cast 'Gravity Normalization' spells, but they charged a premium fee for emergency services. The bill was split among the patrons, adding significantly to the tab.

The brewery responsible for the stout has issued a recall, stating that a batch of levitation yeast was accidentally added during fermentation. They are offering refunds in the form of coupons for their next product, 'Earthshaker Ale', which reportedly makes drinkers weigh five hundred pounds. Durnan has decided to keep the remaining keg locked in a lead-lined cellar, though rumors suggest he plans to sell tickets for the next floating night. For now, the Yawning Portal remains grounded, but customers are advised to check their feet before ordering. The incident has sparked a trend in other taverns, with some attempting to replicate the effect using illegal potions, leading to several incidents of patrons floating away into the night sky.

articles.title: Annual Pet Contest Ends in Polymorph Chaos
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: Judges accidentally cast wild magic. Cats are now dogs, dogs are now chickens. Owners distraught but amused.
articles.content:
The annual Waterdeep Pet Extravaganza, typically a dignified affair showcasing the finest mastiffs and faerie dragons, descended into chaos yesterday when the head judge accidentally triggered a wild magic surge while scoring a particularly cute puppy. The surge released a wave of polymorph energy that swept through the arena, instantly swapping the species of every animal present. Cats found themselves barking, dogs began meowing, and a prized show chicken suddenly grew scales and breathed a tiny puff of smoke. The owners were left staring at their pets in confusion, trying to determine if their beloved companion was still the same soul inside a different body.

Panic was averted when the organizers assured the crowd that the effects were temporary, lasting only until the next moonrise. However, this did not stop the confusion. Several owners struggled to feed their pets, as the dietary needs had suddenly changed. A woman was seen trying to feed milk to a dog that now believed it was a cat, while a wizard struggled to keep his new pet rock from rolling away. The betting rings, which had been established on which pet would win the beauty contest, had to be suspended as the criteria for judging became impossible to apply. How does one judge the grace of a turtle that thinks it is a hawk?

Some owners actually preferred the new arrangements. A farmer whose dog was notoriously lazy was delighted to find it had turned into a workhorse, even if it was the size of a terrier. Conversely, a nobleman whose cat was a skilled mouser was dismayed to find it had become a goldfish, albeit one that could walk on land. The event organizers are now facing a lawsuit from a participant whose hamster turned into a owlbear and ate the judging table. The insurance company has declared the event an 'Act of Magic' and is refusing to pay damages.

By evening, most pets had reverted to their original forms, though some lingering effects remain. Several dogs are still chasing laser pointers with unnatural intensity, and a few cats have developed a fear of vacuum cleaners that they did not possess before. The head judge has been suspended pending a review of his spell components, though he claims the surge was caused by a spectator sneezing during a critical somatic component. The Gazette recommends all pet owners keep a dispel magic scroll handy for future events. Next year's contest will be held in an anti-magic field, though organizers worry this will make the pets too boring to watch.

articles.title: Weather Wizards Union Go on Strike
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: No rain permits issued this week. Farmers angry. Clouds are forming shapes of picket signs.
articles.content:
The Guild of Meteoromancers has officially gone on strike, leaving the skies over the Kingdom of Cormyr in a state of unpredictable limbo. The wizards, who are responsible for summoning rain, clearing storms, and ensuring optimal sunlight for crops, are protesting new regulations that require them to file paperwork for every cloud they create. The union representative, a storm sorcerer with a voice like thunder, stated that the bureaucratic burden is preventing them from focusing on their arcane duties. As a result, no rain permits have been issued this week, and farmers are watching their fields dry up under an unseasonably hot sun.

The strike has led to some creative forms of protest. Clouds over the capital city have been observed forming shapes resembling picket signs and angry faces. Lightning bolts are striking only near government buildings, carefully avoiding private property to minimize liability while still making a point. The King's advisors are scrambling to find replacement casters, but qualified weather wizards are rare and highly unionized. Attempts to hire freelance sorcerers from the distant lands of Zakhara have been thwarted by visa issues and the high cost of importing humidity.

Farmers are furious, claiming that the strike threatens the harvest and could lead to food shortages by winter. Some have resorted to praying to nature deities, but the clerics report that the gods are also busy filling out paperwork. A group of druids has offered to help, but they refuse to work under the same conditions as the wizards, citing a longstanding rivalry between arcane and divine weather control. The situation has created a black market for rain, with shady alchemists selling bottled mist at exorbitant prices. The city guard has cracked down on these sales, citing health codes regarding bottled precipitation.

Negotiations are scheduled to take place in a neutral location, likely a cloud castle floating above the dispute. The wizards are demanding better wand stipends and hazard pay for dealing with hurricane seasons. The government is holding firm, arguing that the weather is a public resource and should not be held hostage. Meanwhile, citizens are advised to carry umbrellas at all times, not for rain, but to protect themselves from falling debris as the wizards test their new storm spells near the palace. The Gazette will continue to monitor the situation, though our own weather forecasters have joined the picket line. Expect sunshine with a chance of bureaucracy.

articles.title: Adventurer's Guild Mandates Ramps for Gelatinous Cubes
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: New dungeon safety laws require wide corridors. Delvers complain stealth is impossible with construction crews everywhere.
articles.content:
The Adventurer's Guild of Waterdeep has issued a controversial new decree requiring all active dungeons to be compliant with the Arcane Accessibility Act. The legislation, championed by the Gelatinous Cube Union, mandates that all corridors must be at least ten feet wide to accommodate mobility-impaired monsters and that all pit traps must include a gently sloping ramp for easy exit. Dungeon masters across the realm are furious, claiming that the structural integrity of their lairs is being compromised by the installation of elevators and handrails in ancient crypts.

Leading the charge against the regulations is veteran delver Thorin Stonegrump, who argues that the essence of dungeon crawling is the risk of death by narrow hallway. He stated, "How am I supposed to feel the thrill of danger when there is a clearly marked emergency exit sign glowing above every ogre's chamber? The ambiance is ruined." The Guild counters that too many adventurers were getting stuck in corners while fleeing from beholders, leading to excessive insurance claims. They argue that a safe dungeon is a profitable dungeon, as more parties will be willing to sign contracts if they know the restroom facilities are wheelchair accessible.

Construction crews have been spotted in the Undermountain, hammering away at ancient stone to widen passages. This has inadvertently awakened several sleeping liches who were disturbed by the noise of power tools. The liches have joined the protest, claiming that the vibrations are cracking their bone structures. Meanwhile, the Gelatinous Cubes are celebrating, having successfully negotiated for better lighting conditions so they can be seen more clearly by potential victims. They argue that surprise attacks are unfair if the victim has poor darkvision.

The economic impact is already being felt. Contractors specializing in magical masonry are charging double rates for emergency retrofitting. Some dungeon owners are choosing to close their doors rather than comply, leading to a shortage of low-level quests for novice adventurers. Many are now forced to practice combat on training golems in the city parks, which lack the visceral terror of a real goblin ambush. The Guild has promised to review the regulations next quarter, but for now, adventurers are advised to bring hard hats along with their swords. The first dungeon to be fully certified is the Tomb of Mild Inconvenience, where all traps deal non-lethal bludgeoning damage.

articles.title: Mass Recall Issued for 'Whiner +1' Magic Swords
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: The blades scream constantly during combat. Manufacturers offer free mute spells. Paladins return them in droves.
articles.content:
Steel & Whine Inc., a prominent manufacturer of enchanted weaponry based in Lantan, has issued a voluntary recall for their latest model of magic longswords, the 'Whiner +1'. The weapon, marketed as a blade that provides tactical advice during battle, has been found to have a critical defect: the tactical advice never stops. Users report that the swords scream complaints about the wielder's form, the weather, and the ethical implications of violence continuously, even during stealth missions. Several rogues have been caught because their sword started critiquing their lockpicking technique in a booming voice.

The recall affects all serial numbers starting with 'MOAN-2024'. The company is offering a free retrofit service where a court wizard will cast a permanent 'Silence' spell on the weapon's core. However, customers must ship the blade back at their own expense, which is problematic given that the swords refuse to be sheathed until they finish their monologue. Many owners have resorted to wrapping the blades in multiple layers of cloth and stuffing them into bags of holding to muffle the noise. Some paladins have returned the weapons entirely, claiming that the constant negativity is affecting their divine connection.

One affected customer, a barbarian named Krag, stated, "I tried to rage, but the sword told me that anger is a construct of the patriarchy and that I should try journaling instead. It ruined my momentum." The company insists that the feature was intended to promote mindfulness among warriors, but admits that the volume settings were calibrated incorrectly. Instead of a whisper, the voices manifest at the decibel level of a thunderclap. This has led to several incidents of adventurers going deaf after a prolonged skirmish with a group of goblins.

Consumer protection wizards are investigating whether the swords constitute a cursed item under federal law. If classified as cursed, the company could face heavy fines and be forced to offer full refunds in platinum pieces. In the meantime, second-hand shops are flooded with the weapons, sold at a discount with a warning label attached. Some bards have purchased them to use as backing vocals, though they complain the sword is always off-key. Steel & Whine Inc. has released a statement apologizing for the disturbance and promising that their next model, the 'Passive-Aggressive Dagger +2', will only criticize the wielder internally.

articles.title: College of Valor Accused of Song Plagiarism
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: Famous ballad found to be written by a mute kobold. Dean resigns. Students demand tuition refund in gold.
articles.content:
A major scandal has rocked the prestigious College of Valor in Silverymoon after it was revealed that their anthem, 'The Ballad of the Heroic Dawn', was actually written by a mute kobold named Squeak who was paid in chicken scraps. The discovery was made when a linguist from Candlekeep recognized the melody in an ancient draconic dialect used by kobold tribes to signal danger. The Dean of the College, a high elf known for his perfect pitch and questionable ethics, has resigned amidst accusations of cultural appropriation and artistic fraud.

Students are outraged, claiming that their degrees are now worthless because the institution's reputation is tarnished. They have gathered in the courtyard, playing lutes out of tune to protest the administration. They are demanding a full refund of their tuition, payable in solid gold bars rather than the promissory notes the college usually issues. The alumni association has threatened to revoke donations, which fund the magical lighting in the practice rooms. Without this funding, students may be forced to practice in the dark, leading to an increase in accidental tripping hazards during performance exams.

Squeak the kobold has become an unexpected celebrity, featured on the cover of 'Bards Monthly' magazine. He communicates through a series of chirps and gestures, which translators say convey a message of forgiveness but also a demand for better working conditions for kobold composers. He has been offered a recording contract by a gnome-run label, but his manager says he is considering retiring to a quiet cave. The College has attempted to settle out of court by offering Squeak a lifetime supply of shiny objects and a tenure track position, but he reportedly used the contract to light a fire.

The incident has sparked a wider investigation into the origins of other popular songs. Several hits by famous bards are now under scrutiny for potential kobold involvement. The Guild of Musicians is drafting new regulations requiring all composers to sign an affidavit swearing they did not coerce a small reptilian humanoid into writing their hits. Meanwhile, the College of Valor is struggling to recruit new faculty, as most qualified bards do not want to be associated with the scandal. Classes have been cancelled for the week while the board searches for an interim Dean, preferably one who cannot sing.

articles.title: Healing Potions Found to be Watered Down
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: Alchemists caught adding tap water to red vials. Adventurers report feeling only slightly less dead. Investigation ongoing.
articles.content:
The Alchemist Guild of Waterdeep is facing a crisis of confidence after an investigation revealed that several major potion manufacturers have been diluting their Healing Potions with ordinary tap water. Adventurers across the realm reported that drinking the vials after combat resulted in only a minor tingling sensation rather than the immediate closure of wounds. One barbarian claimed he drank three potions after falling off a cliff and still felt like he had been hit by a carriage. The Guild has launched an internal audit to determine the scope of the contamination.

The scandal came to light when a quality control inspector noticed that the specific gravity of the potions was lower than the standard required by arcane law. Further testing revealed that some batches contained up to sixty percent municipal water, sourced from the city's fountains. The manufacturers claim that the water was 'blessed' by a low-level cleric, which they argued should count towards the magical potency. The Guild disagrees, stating that a blessing does not replace the need for actual red moss and phoenix blood, the key ingredients for genuine healing magic.

Consumers are demanding refunds and medical compensation. Several adventurers are still hospitalized because the fake potions wore off mid-battle, causing their injuries to reopen. The city guard has raided three major apothecaries, seizing crates of pink-tinted water labeled as 'Extra Strength'. The owners are facing charges of fraud and endangerment, punishable by transformation into a toad. The market for healing potions has crashed, with prices plummeting as buyers lose trust in the sealed vials.

In response, some parties are returning to traditional healing methods, hiring clerics to lay on hands instead of relying on consumables. However, clerics are reporting a backlog of appointments, as there are only so many hands to go around. The Guild has issued a new certification seal that glows when held near a genuine potion, but counterfeiters are already working on ways to replicate the light effect using phosphorescent fungi. Until trust is restored, the Gazette recommends testing potions on a captured goblin before consuming them yourself. Health officials warn that drinking unidentified red liquid may cause temporary hallucinations or excessive burping.

articles.title: Mimic Infestation Hits Suburban Mailboxes
articles.author: Gobwiz Quillnib
articles.excerpt: Residents bitten while checking bills. Postal service issues armored gloves. Mimics demand stamp payment.
articles.content:
A quiet neighborhood in the suburbs of Waterdeep is on high alert after a swarm of mimics was discovered inhabiting the residential mailboxes. The creatures, typically found in dungeons guarding treasure chests, have adapted to suburban life by disguising themselves as standard issue wooden post boxes. Residents report opening their boxes to retrieve bills only to be snapped at by rows of sharp teeth. The local postal service has suspended delivery to the affected street until the creatures can be removed by a professional pest control wizard.

The infestation began when a wizard moved into the neighborhood and accidentally transmutated his own mailbox while practicing shape-changing spells. The spell spread to neighboring boxes through a shared wooden fence, causing a chain reaction. The mimics have proven difficult to remove, as they blend in perfectly with the wood grain and only move when no one is looking. Several mail carriers have required healing potions after being bitten on the fingers. The union is threatening to strike unless they are provided with plate mail gloves and hazard pay.

Interestingly, the mimics have developed demands. They refuse to release mail unless they are fed a steady diet of junk mail and catalogs. They seem to have a particular taste for parchment written in ink rather than printed material. Some residents have started feeding them old spellbooks to keep them docile, though this is illegal under the Conservation of Magical Knowledge Act. The city council is debating whether to classify the mimics as pets or pests, a distinction that carries significant tax implications.

Pest control wizards are attempting to lure the mimics away with larger chests filled with fake gold, but the creatures have become savvy to such tricks. One mimic was caught trying to open a neighbor's mailbox to steal their mail, suggesting they are evolving socially. The Postal Service has issued a statement advising residents to use a stick to open their boxes until further notice. They are also exploring the use of golem carriers who do not feel pain. Until the infestation is cleared, residents are advised to switch to digital communication via crystal ball, though the mimics are reportedly learning how to hack those as well.
